3.happening: We compare the lengths of s and t.
3.why: Two strings of different lengths can never be anagrams of each other.
3.invariant: Anagrams must use exactly the same number of characters.

4.happening: The lengths differ, so we return False right away.
4.why: No further work is needed once a disqualifying difference is found.
4.invariant: A False result means t is not an anagram of s.

5.happening: We create counts as an empty dictionary.
5.why: The map will record how many times each character appears in s.
5.invariant: counts holds the frequency of every character seen so far in s.

6.happening: Index i advances to the next character of s.
6.why: Every character of s must be tallied exactly once.
6.invariant: All characters before index i have already been counted.

7.happening: We read the current character ch from s.
7.why: This is the character whose tally we are about to increase.
7.invariant: ch is the character of s at index i.

8.happening: We add one to the tally for character '{ch}'.
8.why: The map must reflect exactly how many of each character s contains.
8.invariant: counts sums to i + 1 once this line finishes.

9.happening: Index j advances to the next character of t.
9.why: Each character of t should cancel one tally recorded from s.
9.invariant: All characters before index j have already been cancelled.

10.happening: We read the current character d from t.
10.why: This is the character whose tally we are about to reduce.
10.invariant: d is the character of t at index j.

11.happening: We check whether '{d}' was ever counted from s.
11.why: If t contains a character s never had, the strings cannot match.
11.invariant: A missing key means t has a character absent from s.

12.happening: Character '{d}' is missing from the map, so we return False.
12.why: t contains a letter that s does not, which rules out an anagram.
12.invariant: A False result means t is not an anagram of s.

13.why: Each character of t should remove one of the occurrences counted from s.
13.invariant: A tally reaching zero means s and t used that character equally often.

14.happening: We check whether the tally for '{d}' went below zero.
14.why: A negative tally means t uses this character more times than s did.
14.invariant: Balanced anagrams never drive any tally below zero.

15.happening: The tally for '{d}' is negative, so we return False.
15.why: t repeats a character more often than s contains it.
15.invariant: A False result means t is not an anagram of s.

16.happening: Every tally cancelled cleanly, so we return True.
16.why: Matching counts for every character proves t is a rearrangement of s.
16.invariant: A True result means s and t contain the same characters with the same frequencies.

init.why: The algorithm needs somewhere to record the frequency of each character in s.
init.invariant: No character has been counted yet.

check.happening: Test a condition against the current data.
check.why: Length, presence, and non-negativity checks each catch a way the strings could differ.
check.invariant: A failed check ends the method with False.

loop.happening: Advance to the next character to process.
loop.why: Both strings are scanned once from left to right.
loop.invariant: Characters already passed have been fully accounted for.

update.happening: Read a character and adjust its tally.
update.why: Counting up for s and down for t makes every character cancel to zero when the strings match.
update.invariant: The map always reflects s's counts minus the part of t processed so far.

return.why: The scan has reached a decisive result.
return.invariant: The returned boolean states whether t is an anagram of s.
